coredata.dc:title: Naked oats-derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Li, Xiaofeng
Li, Yanyan
Wen, Xiangping
Li, Junfen
Choi, Martin M.F.
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: We report a green and facile strategy for fabrication of dual-emission carbon nanodots (CDs), and demonstrate their applications in ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging. Dual-emission CDs have been prepared by pyrolysis and microwave treatment of naked oats, providing a novel way for the production of dual luminance CDs without the request of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Their intriguing dual fluorescence behavior is observed when the excitation wavelength is between 250 and 310nm. The well-resolved dual emission bands manifest excitation and temperature dependence. The obtained CDs were applied as a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for precise and quantitative detection of Al3+ ions and pH values, and as optical nanoprobes for cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescence
Ratiometric sensing
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
The synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of CDs from pyrolysis of naked oats and the PL enhancement by microwave irradiation, along with photographs of the corresponding samples under UV light.
(a) TEM image of CDs. Inset: corresponding size distribution histogram. (b) HRTEM image of CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CDs. (b) C1s and (c) O1s spectra of CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(a) Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. (b) Excitation dependent PL spectra of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs (emission wavelengths of 347nm and 428nm). (b) Fluorescence decay of CDs at 347nm and 428nm (excitation at 310nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs solutions (0.15mgmL−1) under pH 5.0 condition at 310nm excitation in the presence of different concentrations of Al3+ (1–13): 0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 175, 200, 250, and 300μM. (b) Plot of I 428/I 347 versus Al3+ concentrations. (c) Fluorescence responses of the CDs solution (0.15mgmL−1) to different metal ions (300μM for all the metal ions) under pH 5.0 condition at 310nm excitation. (d) Selectivity of CDs solution for Al3+ over other ionic species. The black bars represent the addition of metal ions (300μM for all the metal ions). The red bars represent the subsequent addition of 300μM Al3+ to the CDs solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs solutions (0.15mgmL−1) with different pH values (1–11): 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 8.0, 9.5, and 11.0 under 310nm excitation. (b) Sigmoidal fitting of the pH-dependent I 428/I 347. Inset: the good linearity in the pH range of 3.0–6.0.
Confocal fluorescence images of 786-0 cells incubated with 0.2mg/mL CDs for 24h under (a) bright field and (b) 488nm excitation. (c) The merged image of a and b.
Detection results of Al3+ in river water with the proposed method and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry.

row.entry: Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry
Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry

head.ce:title: Naked oats-derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong SAR, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a green and facile strategy for fabrication of dual-emission carbon nanodots (CDs), and demonstrate their applications in ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging. Dual-emission CDs have been prepared by pyrolysis and microwave treatment of naked oats, providing a novel way for the production of dual luminance CDs without the request of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Their intriguing dual fluorescence behavior is observed when the excitation wavelength is between 250 and 310nm. The well-resolved dual emission bands manifest excitation and temperature dependence. The obtained CDs were applied as a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for precise and quantitative detection of Al3+ ions and pH values, and as optical nanoprobes for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles have a tremendous impact on the advancement of a wide range of fields including electronics, photonics, energy, catalysis, and medicine [1]. To date, typical photoluminescent (PL) particles have been developed from compounds of lead, cadmium, gold, silver and silicon [2–5]. But these materials also have raised concerns over potential toxicity, environmental harm and high cost [5,6].

Carbon nanodots (CDs), fascinating fluorescent nanoparticles, are generally defined as carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10nm and considered to consist of an amorphous or crystalline core with predominant sp2 carbon and an oxidized carbon surface with carboxyl group [7]. By virtue of their low cost, high aqueous solubility, low photobleaching, non-blinking, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have gradually become superior alternatives to other fluorescent nanoparticles [8]. These distinct benefits of CDs make them great promise for a variety of practical applications, such as bioimaging [9], chemical sensor and biosensor [10–12], drug delivery [13], gene delivery [14], dye sensitizers [15], catalysis [16,17], fluorescent ink [18], and mimetics peroxidase [19]. Currently, CDs are typically synthesized by two types of strategies: top-down and bottom-up routes. Top-down approaches involve laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation of graphite [20,21], electrochemical treatment of multiwalled carbon nanotubes [22], and chemical oxidation of commercially activated carbon [23]. Bottom-up methods include pyrolysis [24,25], wet oxidation [26,27], hydrothermal synthesis [28–31], and microwave-assisted synthesis [18,32] with all sorts of carbon precursors, such as plant leaf [24], glycerol [25], hair fiber [26], tire soot [27], Bombyx mori silk [28], soy milk [29], saccharides [30,31], citric acid [18], and amino acid [32], etc.

During the past few years, many attempts have been made toward lowering the cost, simplifying operation, improving quantum yield (QY), or realizing larger-scale production of CDs. However, tuning the PL behavior of CDs has been less explored. Giannelis et al. [33] for the first time developed dual emission N-doped CDs by the controlled pyrolysis of citric acid and ethanolamine (1:3 molar ratio) at different temperatures and also elaborated the PL mechanism of the CDs arising from the excitation state of the core domains and the surface chemistry. Since then, dual-emission CDs, momentous supplement to single fluorescence CDs, have attracted wide attention because of their potential applications in chemical sensing and biological tracing. Wang et al. [34] proposed an ingenious strategy for fabrication of dual emission cross-linked fluorescent CDs by mixing acetic acid, water, and diphosphorus pentoxide, and Tang et al. [35] attributed dual fluorescence bands of as-prepared CDs to the emission from the core and the surface states. Xiong et al. [36] reported novel long-chain cationic surfactant (nonylphenol polyethylene glycol quaternary ammonium salt (NPEQ)) coated CDs through ionic interaction and discovered that the aqueous solution of obtained CDs presented distinct dual emission peaks ascribing to a partial ionization of NPEQ molecules from CDs. Hu et al. [37] applied an effective approach to prepare dual emission N-doped CDs by the pyrolysis of PAN (Poly Acrylonitrile)@PMMA (Poly Methyl methacrylate) core–shell nanoparticles at different temperatures and clarified the PL mechanism of the CDs originating from the amide groups (or imine groups) of the surface and the amorphous carbonaceous core. Nevertheless, all these methods suffer from some drawbacks like the requirement of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Thus the production of dual-emission CDs toward green and facile fabrication is a challenging but worthy issue.

In this contribution, we present a green and facile approach to obtain dual-emission CDs from naked oats by pyrolysis and microwave treatment for the first time. Compared with the previous works, our devised method avoids the request of tedious synthetic methodology or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. Remarkably, the as-synthesized CDs display excitation and temperature tunable dual luminescence and favorable stability under UV radiation or long-term storage. Coupled with the characteristic PL properties and enhanced stability, the as-synthesized CDs have been successfully utilized to ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Al3+ ions and pH values, and confocal fluorescent microscopic images of human renal clear cell carcinoma (786-0) cells.

AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, HgCl2, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China). NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Naked oats were purchased from local market (Taiyuan, China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of naked oats. In a typical run, a crucible loaded with 5g of naked oats was transferred into a muffle furnace, and pyrolyzed at 400°C for 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black products were mechanically ground to fine powders. 1g of the resultant sample was dispersed in 5mL DDI water and magnetically stirred to form a black solution. The CDs aqueous solution were collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 12 000rpm for 10min.
PL enhancement of CDs was achieved by further microwave treatment. Typically, the obtained CDs aqueous solution was treated in a domestic microwave oven (700W) for 12min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was carried out in a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size distribution of CDs was performed by counting over 100 particles. Fluorescent photographs of CDs under UV light of 365nm were operated with ZF-2 ultraviolet analyzer for three purposes from Shanghai City Anting electronic instrument factory. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Nicolet Magna 550 spectrometer using a resolution of 4cm−1. The sample with 1mg diluted by KBr (ratio 1:200) was pressed into the disc. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer from Shimadzu company using 300W Al Kα radiation. The base pressure was about 3×10−9 mbar and the binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.6eV from adventitious carbon. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (China). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). A nanosecond hydrogen flash lamp was used as the excitation light source in the determination of Fluorescence lifetime.

The relative QY (Φ) of the CDs was calculated using the equation of
Φ x
=
Φ
s t d
I x
A
s t d
η x 2
/ (
I
s t d
A x
η
s t d
2
)
. The optical densities were measured on Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. In the equation, I
x and I
std are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs and the standard, and A
x and A
std are the optical densities (OD) of the CDs and the standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was chosen as a standard with a quantum yield Φ
std
=0.54 at 360nm. η
x and η
std are the refractive index of the CDs and the standard, respectively. The absorbencies of all the samples in 1.0cm cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

The detection of Al3+ ions was performed at room temperature in 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (containing 150mM NaCl, pH 5.0). In a typical run, 30μL of CDs dispersion (1.6mg/mL) was added into 300μL of PBS, followed by the addition of Al3+ standard with various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

River water sample was treated by ultrafiltration with 10kD ultrafiltration membrane, spiked with standard Al3+ ion at different concentration levels, diluted within the working linear range, and analyzed with the proposed method and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES).

The detection of pH values was performed in 10mM PBS (containing 150mM NaCl) with various pH values. In a typical run, 30μL of CDs dispersion (1.6mg/mL) was added into 300μL of PBS with various pH values. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature. The measurements were conducted in triplicate.

For the cell cytotoxicity text, 786-0 cells were first plated on a Costar 96-well tissue-culture cluster and cultured at 37°C with 5% CO2 in air for 3h to adhere cells onto the surface. The well without cells and treatment with CDs was taken as a zero set. The medium was then changed with 100μL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS containing CDs, and the cells were allowed to grow for another 24h. At least five parallel samples were performed in each group. Cells without treatment with CDs were taken as a control. After adding 20μL of 5.0mg/mL MTT reagent into every well, the cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, and then 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca. 10min at room temperature. The OD of the mixture was measured at 490nm with a SunRisemicroplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The cell viability was estimated using the equation of Cell Viability (%)=(OD
Treated /OD
Control )×100%, where OD
Control and OD
Treated were obtained in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively.

786-0 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The CDs aqueous solution (140μL, 1.6mg/mL) was added to the culture medium (1.0mL) at 0.2mg/mL final concentration. After incubation for 24h, the 786-0 cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0mL each) with pH 7.4 PBS (comprising 137mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl, 8.0mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0mM KH2PO4), and kept in PBS for optical imaging by a Olympus FV1000 confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with 40× objective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The successful preparation of dual-emission CDs was carried out by using naked oats as the starting materials via pyrolysis and microwave treatment (Fig. 1 ). Naked oats are an agricultural crop with yellow and lanky grain and abound with starch, protein, and fat, which may serve as widespread, easily available, low-cost, and green natural carbon sources for large-scale fabrication of dual emission CDs. After pyrolysis of naked oats, obtained CDs show weak blue luminance. The PL enhancement is observed by further microwave treatment of obtained CDs. The rapid treatment using microwave irradiation could contribute to uniform size of CDs and further increase of the functional groups on the surface, resulting in the better PL performance [29]. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the respective QY values after the preliminary pyrolysis and subsequent microwave treatment are found to be 1.2% and 3.0%, comparable with those of the reported luminescent CDs [29,38]. As-prepared CDs freely disperse in water with transparent appearance. The pH value of the fresh CDs is ca. 5.7 because of the formation of carboxyl groups.

The morphology and size distribution of CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, CDs are spherical in morphology and well dispersed. The inset in Fig. 2a demonstrates that the size distribution of the CDs is relatively small and the majority falls within the range from 7 to 11nm. Based on the statistical analysis of above one hundred particles, the average diameter of the CDs is 8.64±0.84nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2b) shows a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.301nm which may be attributable to the (002) facet of graphite [22].

The surface functional groups and element states of CDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS analysis of the elemental composition of CDs demonstrates that these CDs are mainly composed of C and O element (the atom ratio of C:O is 1.05:1). The XPS spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 3a) shows three peaks at 282.5, 290.0, and 529.6eV. The first two correspond to C1s and another one corresponds to O1s. Other peaks at 130.6, 188.8, 303.5, and 374.4eV belong to elements such as P, B, Pr and Ag, respectively. These elements come from the trace elements in naked oats. The C1s spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 3b) indicates the presence of three types of carbon bonds: CC (284.7eV), CC (285.8eV), and OCO (288.9, 292.9, and 295.7eV). In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), two peaks at 531.4 and 532.7eV are ascribed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. The XPS results confirm that the synthesized CDs are functioned with carboxylic moieties. We also used FTIR to identify the functional groups of CDs. In FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3d), the peaks at 3426 and 3151cm−1 are attributed to the OH and CH stretching vibrations, the peaks at about 1634cm−1 and 1400cm−1 indicated the existence of COO−, the peaks at 1114 and 1165cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching vibrations, and the peak at 952cm−1 is ascribed to the OH bending vibrations. The functional groups identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS.

The optical properties of CDs are displayed in Figs. 4 and 5 . As shown in the UV absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a), CDs exhibit a strong absorption peak at 247nm together with a shoulder around 279nm. The peak at 247nm is ascribed to π–π * transition of the CC bands of localized sp2 clusters in the core, whereas the shoulder is due to n–π * transition of the CO bands of carboxyl group on the surface of CDs. The fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 4a) shows two peaks at 347 and 428nm when excited at 310nm. To further explore their optical properties, the excitation- and temperature-dependent emission spectra are recorded in Fig. 4b and c. The PL spectra of CDs under excitation wavelength from 250 to 310nm were measured (Fig. 4b). Interestingly, dual emission bands, UV band and blue band, are observed in the excitation wavelength range which can be attributed to the emission from the core and the surface states [33,35,37], respectively. It should be noted that with increasing excitation wavelength PL intensity changes obviously. UV band increases continuously when excitation wavelength changes from 250 to 290nm but decreases drastically with longer exciting wavelength of 300 and 310nm probably because the excitation energy is not sufficient to drive the π–π * transition. Meanwhile, with increasing excitation wavelength from 250 to 310nm blue band increases constantly after being stable. What's more, the PL intensity is also dependent on temperature (Fig. S1). It is found that the intensity of dual luminance exhibits a monotonous decrease upon increasing temperature. This indicates that CDs exhibit a metallic-nanocluster-like temperature behavior due to the weaker electron–phonon interaction [35].

The fluorescence excitation spectra (Fig. 5a) of the CDs solution recorded at 347nm and 428nm show two different transition bands centered at 295nm and 315nm, respectively. Fluorescence lifetime is the characteristic period that the CDs remain in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state. Typically measured in nanoseconds, fluorescent lifetime is an intrinsic property of CDs depending on the nature of the fluorescent sites and the environment. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs is assessed by fluorescent decay curves. As seen in Fig. 5b, the fluorescent decay curves of the CDs solution are recorded at 347nm and 428nm under 310nm excitation. The decay curves are fitted using exponential functions Y(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation: Y(t)= A 1exp(−t/τ 1), where A 1 is the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1. The fluorescence lifetimes recorded at 347nm and 428nm were 3.92±0.04ns and 8.59±0.19ns, respectively. These results indicate that the two well-resolved emission bands under single excitation wavelength are from different electronic transition processes and might produce different fluorescent signal responses to external physical or chemical stimuli, showing that as-prepared CDs could be used as ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors on the basis of this dual fluorescence. The stability of the as-prepared CDs was investigated. After UV excitation for two hours or storage for two months (Fig. S2), the two fluorescence bands show fewer fluctuations and the intensity ratio of the two bands is nearly constant under the same test conditions. The results reveal that the CDs have excellent stability, making them promising for real applications.

Aluminium (Al), as the most abundant metallic element in the earth's crust, has been found to be linked with health problems like Alzheimer's disease [39], myopathy, osteomalacia [40], Parkinson's disease [41], and even breast cancer [42]. The investigation of reliable detection methods for Al3+ ions has become indispensible. Recently, some ratiometric fluorescent sensors for detection of Al3+ ions have been reported [43–46]. The development of these probes for Al3+ ions is limited by the design of new dual-emission organic fluorophores, such as naphthalimide-quinoline [43], Alizarin red S-meso-tetra(N-methyl-4-pyridyl)porphine tetratosylate salt [44], quinoline-coumarin [45], and pyrene-pyrimidine [46]. Organic fluorophores based probes always suffer from photobleaching and require tedious synthetic methodology. Unique characteristics of CDs, together with their facile preparation, make them promising candidates for organic fluorophores in chemical sensing.

The Al3+ sensing capacity of the as-prepared CDs was investigated in the range of 0–300μM under pH 5.0 condition at 310nm excitation. With increasing Al3+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence band at 347nm stays almost unchanged while that at 428nm is gradually quenched. As the Al3+ concentration increases from 2.5 to 300μM, I428 /I 347 linearly decreases from 1.31 to 1.01 (Fig. 6b). The detection limit is estimated to be 1.2μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is 6 times lower than the maximum level (7.4μM) of Al3+ in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO). The time-dependent I 428/I 347 of a CDsAl3+ solution shown in Fig. S3 indicates that only 1min is required to complete the reaction between the CDs and Al3+.

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system, the fluorescence spectra in the presence of representative metal ions were examined under pH 5.0 condition at 310nm excitation, including Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+, as shown in Fig. 6c. Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, and Zn2+ show slight effect on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs while Cd2+, Co2+, K+, and Ni2+ quench moderately the emission of 428nm. Al3+ exhibits distinct quenchment for the emission of 428nm. For Cu2+ and Fe3+, two emission bands are quenched simultaneously and obviously. Al3+ is considered to be hard acid with smaller radius and more positive charges. CDs surface is covered with carboxyl groups. O of carboxyl groups on the CDs surface is considered to be hard base. Thus, the sensor response to Al3+ is due to the strong affinity interaction between hard acid Al3+ and hard base O of carboxyl groups on the CDs surface (Fig. S4). Such interaction gives rise to the fluorescence quenching of CDs. We notice that only the fluorescence band at 428nm suffer quenching in the presence of Al3+. Al3+ exhibits a high affinity for O of carboxyl groups on the CDs surface based on the rule of hard and soft acids and bases, which cause fluorescence quenching at 428nm through electron or energy transfer. On the other hand, Al3+ has no d valence orbital so no coordination between CC band in the CDs core and Al3+ happens, resulting in no fluorescence quenching at 347nm. Two emission bands quenchment produced by Fe3+ were attributed to an inner filter caused by its strong absorption at the mentioned excitation wavelength (Fig. S5). Cu2+ has d valence orbital while CDs core has CC band. Cu2+ can coordinate with CC band in CDs core due to the formation of d-p π bond, causing fluorescence quenching at 347nm through partial transfer of an electron in the excited state to the d orbital of Cu2+. Meanwhile, the functional groups on the CDs surface are responsible for the affinity towards Cu2+, which lead to fluorescence quenching at 428nm through electron or energy transfer. We measured the fluorescence intensity ratio of two emission bands (I 428/I 347) in the presence of various metal ions under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6d, drastic decrease in I 428/I 347 is observed for Al3+ compared with other metal ions, indicating that CDs display excellent selectivity for Al3+ over other cations. Furthermore, no obvious interference is found even with high concentrations (up to 300μM) of other metal ions.

We further apply the proposed methodology for determination of Al3+ in local river water samples. The recoveries using the proposed methodology and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry are 96.3–103.3% and 98.2–101.1%, respectively (Table 1 ). This indicated the potential of the proposed sensor for detection of Al3+ in real samples.

Detection of pH is critically important because it plays a pivotal role in many systems. A lot of researchers have made an intense effort for the development of ratiometric pH probes. Toward this end, various design strategies based on intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [47], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [48], through bond energy transfer (TBET) [49] or other approach [50] have been employed to devise a wide variety of dual-emission or excitation ratiometric fluorescent pH probes with either favorable emission or excitation spectral properties. Despite these efforts, ratiometric probes that can selectively detect protons in aqueous solutions with well-resolved dual emission peaks (emission shift>80nm) are very rare.

The pH sensing capacity of the CDs was studied for a wide range of pH values under 310nm excitation. With increasing pH values from 3.0 to 11.0, the fluorescence band at 347nm remains almost constant while that at 428nm enhances first significantly and then moderately (Fig. 7a), which indicates that increasing pH value has no effect on CDs core but a great effect on CDs surface due to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups (Fig. S6). The deprotonation leads to the formation of delocalized π bond and the increase of n electrons. As shown in Fig. 7b, I 428/I 347 increases linearly and rapidly for the pH values from 3.0 to 6.0 and slowly for the pH values from 6.0 to 11.0 and the sigmoidal fitting yields a pKa value of 4.67, illustrating that the CDs have strong pH-dependent behavior and are suitable for sensing pH value in acid environments. Distinctly, there is a good linearity between I 428/I 347 and pH in the range of 3.0–6.0 (inset of Fig. 7b), according to the linear equation: I 428/I 347 =0.06223+0.2570 pH, R 2 =0.9912. The result indicates that CDs could be used as ratiometric fluorescent pH nanosensor within the range of 3.0–6.0.

The reversibility is very important for pH probe. Fig. S7 show I 428/I 347 of the CDs sensor when the pH is switched between 3.0 and 11.0 for six times. An increase of pH increases I 428/I 347 while by reducing the pH I 428/I 347 is successfully recovered. The reproducibility of the ratio of the emission intensity clearly demonstrates the excellent reversibility and the robustness of the CDs sensor for pH detection.

To explore the potential application of CDs in living cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay in 786-0 cells. In the MTT assay, MTT could be reduced by the active cellular enzymes in the cells to insoluble blue-violet formazan crystals. The quantitative information about the cytotoxicity of CDs would be obtained by the measurement of absorption on the cells. We performed the MTT assay in 786-0 cells with concentrations from 0.05mg/mL to 0.4mg/mL. The results demonstrate that more than 90% of cells are viable (Fig. S8), showing the low toxicity of CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at the concentration of 0.2mg/mL.

To assess the potential application of CDs as a bioimaging probe, 786-0 cells were cultured in the medium containing 0.2mg/mL CDs for 24h at 37°C, and then observed under confocal microscope. Bright-field image after CDs incubation confirms the viability of the cells (Fig. 8a). Confocal fluorescent imaging was performed at an excitation wavelength of 488nm since 250–310nm laser was not available for the confocal microscope. Under 488nm excitation, CDs with green fluorescence emission disperse well within living cells, indicating excellent cell membrane permeability (Fig. 8b). According to the clear cell morphology (Fig. 8a and c), the PL spots are observed only in the cytoplasmic area of the cell while PL intensity of nucleus at the central region is very weak, indicating that the CDs easily penetrate into the cell but rarely enter the nuclei, which avoids genetic disruption. Moreover, analyses of confocal microscopy also indicate that the photo stability of CDs is remarkably high, with no blinking and low photobleaching.

In summary, we have described a green and easy route for fabricating dual-emission CDs with the use of naked oats as the precursor via pyrolysis and microwave treatment. The as-prepared CDs demonstrate excitation and temperature tunable dual luminescence. Also, the CDs have excellent stability such that their PL intensity did not change even after UV radiation for two hours or storage for two months. The as-prepared CDs have been developed as ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors for precise and quantitative sensing of Al3+ ions and pH values by monitoring the intensity ratios of the dual fluorescence bands (I 428/I 347) under 310nm excitation. Moreover, these CDs possess good biocompatibility and can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for bioimaging. Therefore, we demonstrate herein a green and facile strategy to produce dual-emission CDs from widely available green carbon sources for versatile applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175086, 21306108 and 21105060) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China (2013011009–5).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.12.097.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
The cytotoxicity of cadmium based, aqueous phase-synthesized, quantum dots and its modulation by surface coating
Water-soluble silicon quantum dots with wavelength-tunable photoluminescence
Quantum dot bioconjugates for ultrasensitive nonisotopic detection
A toxicologic review of quantum dots: toxicity depends on physicochemical and environmental factors
Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
The production of pH-sensitive photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles by the carbonization of polyethylenimine and their use for bioimaging
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of a crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and graphene
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent carbon nanodots conjugated with folic acid for distinguishing folatereceptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Solution phase synthesis of carbon quantum dots as sensitizers for nanocrystalline TiO2 solar cells
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon nanodots-catalyzed chemiluminescence of luminol: a singlet oxygen-induced mechanism
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon nanodots for sensing, patterning and coding
Facile synthesis of highly emissive carbon nanodots from pyrolysis of glycerol; gram scale production of carbon nanodots/mSiO2 for cell imaging and drug release
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon nanodots with tunable luminescence properties
In vivo imaging of tumour bearing near-infrared fluorescence-emitting carbon nanodots derived from tire soot
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon nanodots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk-natural proteins
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Easy synthesis and imaging applications of cross-linked green fluorescent hollow carbon nanoparticles
Self-assembled long-chain organic ion grafted carbon dot ionic nanohybrids with liquid-like behavior and dual luminescence
Practical access to bandgap-like N-doped carbon dots with dual emission unzipped from PAN@PMMA core–shell nanoparticles
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of cell-permeable multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots without surface passivation reagents
Geographical relation between Alzheimer's disease and aluminum in drinking water
An interesting case of osteomalacia due to antacid use associated with stainable bone aluminum in a patient with normal renal function
Intraneuronal aluminum accumulation in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and Parkinsonism-dementia of Guam
A naphthalimide–quinoline based probe for selective, fluorescence ratiometric sensing of trivalent ions
Design of a dual-signaling sensing system for fluorescent ratiometric detection of Al3+ ion based on the inner-filter effect
A differentially selective sensor with fluorescence turn-on response to Zn2+ and dual-mode ratiometric response to Al3+ in aqueous media
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing and intracellular imaging of Al3+ ions driven by an intramolecular excimer formation of a pyrimidine–pyrene scaffold
Rational design of ratiometric near-infrared fluorescent pH probes with various pKa values, based on aminocyanine
Development of ultrabright semiconducting polymer dots for ratiometric pH sensing
Through-bond energy transfer cassettes with minimal spectral overlap between the donor emission and acceptor absorption: coumarinerhodamine dyads with large pseudo-stokes shifts and emission shifts
Development of pH-responsive fluorescent false neurotransmitters
Synthesis and characterization of ratiometric, pH sensing nanoparticles with covalently attached fluorescent dyes
Delivery and subcellular targeting of dendrimer-based fluorescent pH sensors in living cells
A tunable ratiometric pH sensor based on carbon nanodots for the, quantitative measurement of the intracellular pH of whole cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lihong Shi is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in physical chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors. 7
Xiaofeng Li received BSc degree in chemistry from Shanxi Nomal University in 1999. Now he is a postgraduate in Shanxi University. His research lied in supermolecular chemsitry.
Yanyan Li completed BSc degree in chemistry from Jinzhong University in 2012. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.
Xiangping Wen obtained BSc degree in chemistry from Changzhi University in 2012. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in photochemsity.

Junfen Li is an associate professor of analytical chemistry in Shanxi University. She obtained her PhD degree from Shanxi University in 2005. Her research interests are optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and microscale chemistry.

Martin M.F. Choi is now working in Hong Kong Baptist University as a professor of analytical chemistry. His research focuses on high-performance liquid chromatography separation, biosensors, and chemosensors.

Chuan Dong is a professor of analytical chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his PhD degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of analytical chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her PhD degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta, etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
